design_philosophy.core_loop: Manage economy, draft/train troops, negotiate treaties, and wage war across seasonal cycles to conquer territories and expand empire
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity vs. military ambition—players must balance stable economies, troop maintenance costs, and seasonal agricultural cycles against conquest objectives
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Experience the life and strategic decisions of a historical conqueror (Genghis Khan or rival leaders), managing dynasty through marriages, succession, and territorial expansion
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Emphasis on simulation depth over accessibility; tedious resource management is intentional design forcing players to 'gauge own resources before making decisions' rather than providing narrative-driven experience. Rejects action in favor of deliberative strategy. Multiplayer support (1-4 players) makes it a social negotiation game, not just computational puzzle

difficulty.checkpointing: Seasonal cycle progression; no explicit save/checkpoint system documented
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Players select difficulty level at start of World Conquest mode; leader stat randomization adds variable challenge

progression.upgrade_system: Troop training and recruitment; merchant purchases of resources/products; leader and successor stat allocation
progression.unlock_mechanism: Two campaign modes unlock based on selection: Mongol Conquest (1175 A.D., single-player, fixed protagonist Temujin) vs. World Conquest (1206 A.D., 1-4 players, choice of Mongols/Byzantine/England/Japan)
replayability_hooks: Multiple playable factions with distinct positions (Mongols, Byzantine, England, Japan)
Randomized leader and successor stats affecting long-term strategy
1-4 player multiplayer with dynamic player counts
Two distinct campaign structures with different objectives

world_building_techniques: Historical setting across Eurasia (England to Japan)
Dynasty simulation through marriage arrangement and child succession
Seasonal mechanics reflecting medieval agricultural and military calendars
Leader selection from historical figures (Genghis Khan, Alexios III, Richard, Yoritomo) providing cultural context

faction_indicators: Playable faction choice (Mongols, Byzantine Empire, England, Japan) establishes identity
Treaty system allows diplomatic alignment with other players
Family succession and marriage mechanics create dynasty legacy
danger_signals: Multiplayer economic warfare through resource control
Military conquest as primary win condition
Seasonal troop maintenance costs create pressure for aggressive expansion
environmental_storytelling: Geographic map spanning Eurasia establishes imperial ambition scope
Cyclical seasonal changes reflect historical medieval constraints
Merchant systems imply trade route and economic infrastructure
Troop training mechanics suggest logistical complexity of period warfare
